dateStatements.label: composition
dateStatements.value: circa 1440; in the 1440s and before the 1445 almanac

entities.description: Osbern Bokenham's seventeen-chapter Middle English prose geography of England, created as a reference companion to an English book of saints and translated and adaptively compiled from the final England chapters of Ranulf Higden's Polychronicon Book I.
entities.label: Mappula Anglie
notes: Bokenham identifies himself through the chapter-initial acrostic and a closing Latin couplet.
No physical Polychronicon exemplar has been identified.

rights.basis: The work was composed and attested in the fifteenth century, and the official U-M item carrying its text is marked Public Domain / No Copyright–United States.
rights.component: Fifteenth-century Mappula Anglie textual work
notes: This concerns medieval wording, not modern manuscript photographs, catalog descriptions, or interfaces.

entities.description: An anonymous Middle English prose computistical treatise defining divisions of time, discussing daily and annual cycles and calendar drift, and providing two January–December sequences of daylight and changing day length.
entities.label: Anonymous Harley MS 4011 almanac
notes: The internal statement that 1,444 years had elapsed by the preceding Christmas supports the 1445 date.
Horstmann's German title is editorial; the British Library supplies An Almanac.

rights.basis: The text internally dates its composition to 1445, survives in a fifteenth-century witness, and the official U-M item carrying it is marked Public Domain / No Copyright–United States.
rights.component: Anonymous almanac composed in 1445
notes: This concerns medieval wording, not modern manuscript photography.

entities.description: The Latin geographical source tradition that Bokenham says he drew into fifteen English chapters. Horstmann identifies the source as the final twenty-two chapters of Book I, condensed and supplemented by Bokenham.
entities.label: Ranulf Higden's Polychronicon, Book I, final England chapters
notes: This abstract source-work entity is not a claim that any surviving Polychronicon codex was Bokenham's exemplar.

Request new full-color photography rather than a black-and-white surrogate if that distinction matters.

entities.description: A paper English miscellany cataloged to 1445–1455, containing the Mappula at ff. 144r–163r and the almanac at ff. 164r–169r.
entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 4011
notes: Origin: England.
Horstmann reports a missing leaf at the start of Mappula chapter XV; current images are unavailable for direct codicological verification.

physicalDescription.extent: 189 folios, plus 3 unfoliated paper flyleaves at the beginning and 3 at the end
physicalDescription.format: codex; each leaf now mounted on a paper guard; British Museum binding dated 1885
notes: Gothic cursive script.

The catalog associates William Grauell and William Brewyn with parts of the manuscript; their names or marks occur at ff. 163 and 145 within the target region. Horstmann instead describes two hands and says the later hand continues through the almanac; exact reconciliation remains open.

next_steps: Collate Mappula against critical editions and representative manuscripts of the final England chapters of Polychronicon Book I.
Distinguish direct dependence, common authorities, Bokenham's rearrangements, and local Clare additions.
openQuestions.question: Which Polychronicon exemplar, recension, and additional sources underlie Bokenham's fifteen-chapter adaptation?

next_steps: Compare its computistical definitions, calendar-drift calculation, and paired monthly sequences against manuscript almanac traditions.
Do not infer Bokenham's authorship or Clare composition from adjacency or shared scribal hand alone.
openQuestions.question: Who compiled the 1445 almanac, where was it composed, and what source texts or calculation tables were used?

next_steps: Inspect complete color images or the physical manuscript around ff. 144–169.
Reconcile William Brewyn and William Grauell with Horstmann's hand change, and document the chapter XV lacuna and any historical refoliation.
openQuestions.question: How do Horstmann's two Mappula hands and reported lost leaf at chapter XV correspond to the current catalog's named scribes, paper-guard mounting, foliation, and physical state?

next_steps: Ask the British Library whether an unpublished complete surrogate exists and request or order the complete physical object rather than only target folios.
Include covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, and all leaves where feasible; then map Mappula ff. 144r–163r and the almanac ff. 164r–169r within the supplied sequence.
Secure written permission before redistributing supplied images in Git or elsewhere.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete, reusable color facsimile of Harley MS 4011 be obtained after the former public LARK image route became unavailable?

next_steps: Obtain anthology pp. 352–385 and inspect the Mappula headnote, source statement, text, notes, and any exceptions to the book-wide editorial conventions.
Compare its readings and handling of abbreviations, punctuation, lost material, and embedded Latin verse against Horstmann's 1887 edition and, when available, Harley MS 4011.
openQuestions.question: What text, manuscript or prior edition, selection boundaries, headnote, and chapter-specific editorial interventions underlie the 2019 Mappula presentation?

next_steps: Search U-M CME project documentation and archived technical files for item-level creation metadata.
Compare XML line by line against printed pp. 1, 6–41 and record any keying errors separately from Horstmann's interventions.
openQuestions.question: What production documentation explains the original keying, selection, and quality-control history of the U-M encoding before the recorded 2016 structural revision?

reviewNotes: Mappula and the almanac are separate works. Adjacency and a shared scribal hand do not transfer Bokenham's authorship to the almanac.
Horstmann's 1885 signature dates editorial preparation; the carrier title page establishes publication in 1887.
Internet Archive's generic serial metadata says 1877 and Leipzig, but the inspected volume title page says Englische Studien, X Band, Heilbronn, Gebrüder Henninger, 1887.

Access and rights statements remain component-specific. Public digital access to an 1887 scan does not itself provide a license for the scan derivatives, and British Library catalog-text licensing does not license manuscript photography.
Negative facsimile findings are date-bounded to 2026-07-13.

lineage.summary: The repository XML copies University of Michigan CME item CME00149, a selective encoding of two adjacent Carl Horstmann contributions in Englische Studien 10 (1887). The Mappula stream retains a title and source line from printed p. 1, omits Horstmann's German introduction on pp. 1–5, and then encodes the abbreviation statement and medieval text on pp. 6–34; the anonymous almanac is encoded completely on pp. 34–41. Horstmann transcribed both from British Library, Harley MS 4011: Osbern Bokenham's Mappula Anglie at ff. 144r–163r and an anonymous almanac composed in 1445 at ff. 164r–169r. Mappula translates and adapts the final England chapters of Ranulf Higden's Polychronicon Book I, but no physical source exemplar is known. A complete 1887 journal scan, OCR, item metadata, a supporting Oxford thesis, and the metadata-only official preview for a purchasable 2019 modern Mappula presentation are cached. The British Library currently says manuscript images are unavailable; the former LARK/IIIF route fails, so the manifest records physical consultation and whole-codex paid-reproduction routes without pretending a public manuscript facsimile exists.

catalogSummary.author: Osbern Bokenham (Mappula Anglie); anonymous compiler (almanac)
catalogSummary.date: Mappula circa 1440; almanac composed 1445; both attested in a manuscript cataloged circa 1450; both printed in 1887, explicitly for the first time in the case of Mappula
catalogSummary.region: Mappula composed at Clare, Suffolk, in East Anglia; almanac composition place unknown; shared witness made in England
catalogSummary.title: Mappula Angliae and Ueber Zeitrechnung

catalogingSubject.label: Mappula Angliae and Ueber Zeitrechnung
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the composite University of Michigan item CME00149, not a single medieval work. It joins two adjacent prose units from British Library, Harley MS 4011: Osbern Bokenham's Mappula Anglie (ff. 144r–163r) and an anonymous almanac or computistical treatise composed in 1445 (ff. 164r–169r), together with retained portions of Carl Horstmann's German editorial framing. Shared codex placement and Horstmann's report that the almanac is in the later Mappula hand do not establish common authorship. The physical manuscript, Horstmann's two 1887 printed contributions, their journal carrier, the Michigan encoding, and the repository XML remain separate lineage layers.

contentParts.label: Retained Horstmann editorial framing and apparatus

locators.value: heading/source line p. 1; retained abbreviation note p. 6; notes and closer through p. 34
heading/headnote p. 34; notes and closer through p. 41
DIV1 heads, HEADNOTE, NOTE, DATELINE, SIGNED, CLOSER, and TRAILER elements

notes: Modern editorial framing is not counted as a third medieval work.
contentParts.summary: German contribution headings, source and manuscript lines, the Mappula abbreviation-expansion statement, editorial footnotes, and signed closers retained around the two medieval texts. Horstmann's substantial Mappula introduction on printed pp. 1–5 is omitted except for the relocated heading and source lines.

contentParts.label: Mappula Anglie

locators.value: title/source line p. 1; medieval text and abbreviation note pp. 6–34

notes: Chapter XV begins acephalously in Horstmann's transcription because he reports a lost leaf; the current physical state cannot be checked while images are unavailable.
The epilogue is DIV2 N=2 in the XML, a structural typo contradicted by its own chapter XVII heading and the seventeen-item contents list.
contentParts.summary: Bokenham's seventeen-chapter prose geography of England: a prologue explaining its purpose as a companion to his English saints' book, fifteen chapters translated and adapted from the final England section of Polychronicon Book I, and an epilogue naming the work, describing his compiler-translator role, and completing the authorial acrostic.

contentParts.label: Anonymous Harley MS 4011 almanac

locators.value: DIV1[2]; begins on printed p. 34 before PB 35; PB 35–41; 24 DIV2 month units

notes: The British Library supplies the title An Almanac; Ueber Zeitrechnung is Horstmann's editorial title.
The same hand as the later Mappula section is evidence about scribal production, not authorship.
contentParts.summary: An anonymous prose computistical treatise composed in 1445. It defines divisions and ages of time, describes parts of the day, explains solstices, equinoxes, and accumulated calendar drift, and supplies two annual month sequences for daylight/twilight and the lengthening or shortening of days.

dateStatements.display: Circa 1440; in the 1440s and before the adjacent almanac's 1445 calculation
notes: Horstmann proposes circa 1440; Laferrière places the translation in the 1440s. No false exact normalized interval is supplied for circa dating.
The internal calculation says 1,444 years had elapsed by the preceding Christmas, which supports composition in 1445.
This dates the surviving witness, not necessarily the composition of both abstract works.
Horstmann explicitly describes Mappula as printed here for the first time; the contribution begins on p. 1 and its medieval text on p. 6.

The separately headed almanac contribution begins on p. 34. Horstmann does not explicitly claim publication priority for it, and no earlier printing was identified in the inspected sources; 1887 is therefore recorded as the first verified publication rather than an unqualified first-ever publication.

extent.description: Two prose works in two DIV1 elements: seventeen Mappula chapter divisions and twenty-four almanac month divisions. The Mappula contains two LG elements and three additional isolated L elements, totaling twenty-three embedded Latin verse lines; these do not change the work-level prose classification. The encoding has 53 P, 209 NOTE, and 36 PB elements spanning printed pp. 6–41, with the almanac heading beginning on printed p. 34 before its first PB 35.

formStatements.description: Both medieval works and the retained editorial framing are prose; twenty-three encoded Latin verse lines are embedded quotations or closing material rather than a change in the works' predominant form.

scope.description: Horstmann's edited and annotated 1887 presentation as selectively encoded by Michigan

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: Latin is present although the legacy XML TEXT/@LANG value lists only enm and ger.

scope.description: Latin couplets, quotations, source phrases, and computistical terminology

notes: The XML revision date 8-24-16 is encoding-maintenance metadata, not a composition, manuscript, or publication date.

The legacy TEXT/@LANG value enm ger omits embedded Latin and uses ger rather than the modern bibliographic code deu; normalized language statements preserve the observed layers without rewriting the source.
No public manuscript facsimile was verified on 2026-07-13. That negative finding is date-bounded and does not exclude private, paid, or unindexed reproductions.
Horstmann's Berlin, August 1885 signature dates editorial preparation of the Mappula contribution, not medieval composition or the 1887 volume publication.

next_steps: Collate Mappula chapter by chapter against critical editions and manuscript recensions of Polychronicon Book I.
Separate demonstrable direct source dependence from common encyclopedic authorities and Bokenham's local Clare material.
openQuestions.question: Which Polychronicon exemplar, recension, and any additional written or oral sources underlie Bokenham's omissions, rearrangements, and additions?

next_steps: Compare the definitions, calendar-drift calculation, and two monthly sequences against computus and almanac traditions.
Inspect the manuscript hand and surrounding Harley MS 4011 contents without using scribal identity as automatic proof of authorship.
openQuestions.question: Who compiled the 1445 almanac, where was it composed, and what computistical sources or tables were used?

next_steps: Obtain whole-codex images or repository clarification and inspect ff. 159–163 and neighboring guards.
Reconcile the absent H acrostic initial, textual lacuna, historical foliation, and current catalog description.
openQuestions.question: How does Horstmann's reported lost leaf at the beginning of Mappula chapter XV correspond to the manuscript's current mounting, foliation, and physical state?

next_steps: Consult Simon Horobin's 2008 argument directly and inspect the Abbotsford manuscript's contents, lacunae, language, and authorship evidence.
Keep the attributed legendary distinct from Bokenham's female saints' legends and from the Mappula itself.
openQuestions.question: How securely does the Abbotsford English legendary correspond to the saints' book referenced in Mappula's prologue, and what is its exact textual relationship to Bokenham's other legends?

next_steps: Request or order the complete physical object, including covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, and every recto and verso where handling permits, rather than only ff. 144r–169r.
Record the supplied sequence and map Mappula ff. 144r–163r and the almanac ff. 164r–169r within it.
Obtain written reuse terms before repository redistribution.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete, reusable color facsimile of Harley MS 4011 be obtained and mapped precisely to both works?

notes: Horstmann identifies a Suffolk base altered by two scribal hands; this is not transferred to the anonymous almanac.
The chapter-initial acrostic and closing couplet identify Osbern; chapter XV's lost opening initial is reconstructed within that sequence.
Bokenham calls himself the poor compiler and reorganizes the source into fifteen derived chapters plus a prologue and epilogue.

scope.description: Middle English rendering of the England section at the end of Polychronicon Book I

notes: The work condenses, rearranges, omits, and supplements Higden rather than translating word for word.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous compiler of the Harley MS 4011 almanac
Anonymous compiler of the Harley MS 4011 almanac
notes: Compiler describes the assemblage of computistical definitions, calendar calculations, and month-by-month solar tables without asserting whether all material was newly authored.

scope.description: The two Harley MS 4011 texts printed on pp. 6–41

subjects.label: English regions, islands, roads, rivers, shires, and cities
English laws, kingdoms, bishoprics, peoples, languages, and customs
Marvels, natural resources, and wondrous places of England
Solar year, solstices, equinoxes, daylight, and calendar drift

summaries.value: Mappula Anglie is Osbern Bokenham's Middle English geographical companion to an English saints' book, translating and adapting the final England chapters of Ranulf Higden's Polychronicon Book I into a seventeen-chapter prose treatise on the names, dimensions, resources, marvels, regions, roads, rivers, cities, shires, laws, kingdoms, bishoprics, peoples, languages, and customs of England. The adjacent anonymous 1445 almanac defines divisions of time, explains daily and annual cycles, solstices and equinoxes, and gives two month-by-month sequences for daylight and the lengthening or shortening of days. Horstmann printed both from Harley MS 4011 in 1887, explicitly describing Mappula as appearing in print for the first time; 1887 is the first verified publication of the almanac in the inspected sources.

The XML contains two DIV1 elements and forty-one DIV2 elements. The first DIV1 has seventeen numbered chapters, including prologue and epilogue; its final epilogue is structurally mislabeled N=2 although the heading correctly says chapter XVII. The second DIV1 has introductory computistical prose followed by twenty-four month divisions: a January–December daylight/twilight sequence and a second January–December sequence tracking the year's lengthening and shortening days. Only the first sequence has month headings in the XML.

CME00149 is not a continuous encoding of Horstmann's Mappula contribution. It retains the contribution title and manuscript/source lines from printed p. 1, omits the German introduction on pp. 1–5, then retains the abbreviation note and medieval text on pp. 6–34. The almanac is retained completely on pp. 34–41. Printed p. 34 is shared by the end of Mappula and the beginning of the almanac; p. 41 ends the almanac and begins the next journal article.

notes: A readable composite title supplied from the two retained article headings; it is not asserted as a historical title for one medieval work.
University of Michigan item title; the bracketed conjunction and carrier citation describe the digital item rather than a medieval compilation title.
The title supplied by Bokenham in the epilogue and repeated in the explicit.
Horstmann's printed contribution title, partly retained in the XML.
Horstmann's editorial title; the anonymous medieval text does not supply this German title.

work.author: Osbern Bokenham (Mappula Anglie); anonymous compiler (almanac)
work.dateDisplay: Mappula circa 1440; almanac composed 1445; both attested in a manuscript cataloged circa 1450; both printed in 1887, explicitly for the first time in the case of Mappula
work.regionDisplay: Mappula composed at Clare, Suffolk, in East Anglia; almanac composition place unknown; shared witness made in England

Marvels, natural resources, and wondrous places of England

work.summary: Mappula Anglie is Osbern Bokenham's Middle English geographical companion to an English saints' book, translating and adapting the final England chapters of Ranulf Higden's Polychronicon Book I into a seventeen-chapter prose treatise on the names, dimensions, resources, marvels, regions, roads, rivers, cities, shires, laws, kingdoms, bishoprics, peoples, languages, and customs of England. The adjacent anonymous 1445 almanac defines divisions of time, explains daily and annual cycles, solstices and equinoxes, and gives two month-by-month sequences for daylight and the lengthening or shortening of days. Horstmann printed both from Harley MS 4011 in 1887, explicitly describing Mappula as appearing in print for the first time; 1887 is the first verified publication of the almanac in the inspected sources.
